title: Build a Simple Custom Text Classification AI from Scratch
tagline: A beginner-friendly project that trains a sentiment classifier using scikit‑learn, pandas, and pathlib.
description: In this tutorial you will create a tiny end‑to‑end AI that classifies short text snippets as positive or negative. You’ll start from an empty repository, set up a modern Python project with pyproject.toml, load and clean data with pandas, build a scikit‑learn pipeline, train and evaluate the model, persist it, and finally write a tiny inference script. No classes, no external modules, just plain functions and the latest best practices.
problem_statement: You need a lightweight, reproducible way to classify short user‑generated sentences (e.g., product reviews) as positive or negative without relying on heavyweight deep‑learning frameworks.
end_state: A runnable Python package that can be trained on a CSV file and then used from the command line to predict sentiment for new sentences. The repository includes a test suite and clear documentation.
learning_outcomes: Set up a modern Python project with pyproject.toml and src layout
Load and preprocess data using pandas and pathlib
Create a scikit‑learn Pipeline without writing custom classes
Train, evaluate, and persist a model with joblib
Write a simple CLI inference script
best_practices: Use pyproject.toml for build system and dependencies
Keep all source code under a src/ directory
Prefer functional composition over classes for simple scripts
Leverage pathlib for OS‑independent file handling
Persist models with joblib, not pickle
intro_transcript: Hey everyone, welcome back to the channel! Today we’re going to build a complete, production‑ready AI project from zero to a working command‑line tool – and we’ll do it all with just a handful of Python files. No deep‑learning frameworks, no classes, no complicated boilerplate – just plain functions, pandas, scikit‑learn, and the modern Python tooling you’ll see in real‑world codebases. By the end of this video you’ll have a tiny sentiment classifier that you can train on a CSV of reviews, save to disk, and call from the terminal to predict new sentences. We’ll set up a proper pyproject.toml, use the src layout, add a pytest suite, and follow best practices like pathlib for file paths and joblib for model persistence. Grab your favorite editor, fire up a terminal, and let’s get started – the whole thing should take you about two to three hours, even if you’re brand new to machine learning. Ready? Let’s dive in!

steps.title: Initialize the repository and project configuration
steps.goal: Create a clean folder, add pyproject.toml, and set up src layout.
bullets: Add a minimal pyproject.toml with build system and dependencies
steps.speaker_notes: Alright, first things first – open your terminal and make a new folder called `custom_sentiment_ai`. Inside, run `git init` so we can track our progress. Next, we’ll add a .gitignore that excludes __pycache__, .venv, and any compiled artifacts. The heart of a modern Python project is the pyproject.toml file. I’ll paste a minimal configuration that tells Poetry (or any PEP‑517 builder) which Python version we need and lists pandas, numpy, scikit‑learn, joblib, and pytest as dependencies. Notice we’re using the `[tool.poetry]` section for clarity, but you could also use `[project]` if you prefer the standard library approach. After that, we create a `src` folder where all our code will live, and a `tests` folder for the test suite. This layout keeps import paths tidy and avoids accidental import of the package itself during testing. Once the files are in place, run `poetry install` (or `pip install -e .`) to create a virtual environment and pull in the dependencies. That’s it – we now have a reproducible, version‑controlled foundation for the rest of the tutorial.

code.code:
[tool.poetry]
name = "custom_sentiment_ai"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A tiny sentiment classifier built with scikit‑learn"
authors = ["Your Name <you@example.com>"]
license = "MIT"
readme = "README.md"
packages = [{include = "src"}]

[tool.poetry.dependencies]
python = ">=3.12,<3.13"
pandas = "^2.2"
numpy = "^2.0"
scikit-learn = "^1.5"
joblib = "^1.4"

[tool.poetry.dev-dependencies]
pytest = "^8.2"

[build-system]
requires = ["poetry-core>=1.0.0"]
build-backend = "poetry.core.masonry.api"
code.explanation: Defines project metadata, required Python version, and runtime/dev dependencies.
code.best_practice_notes: Using pyproject.toml centralizes configuration and makes the project PEP‑517 compliant.

steps.title: Add a tiny sample dataset
steps.goal: Create a CSV file with a few labeled sentences for training and testing.
bullets: Use a balanced mix of positive and negative examples
Explain why a small CSV is enough for the demo
steps.speaker_notes: Now we need some data to train our model. For this tutorial we’ll keep it tiny – just eight rows – but the code we write will scale to any size CSV you drop in later. Inside the `src/data` folder, create a file called `sample_reviews.csv`. It will have two columns: `text`, which holds the raw review, and `label`, which is either `positive` or `negative`. I’ll paste the content now. When you run the training script later, pandas will read this file using pathlib, so you don’t have to worry about OS‑specific path separators. Feel free to add more rows later to see how the model improves.

code.code:
text,label
"I love this product, it works great!",positive
"Absolutely terrible, broke after one day.",negative
"Fantastic quality and fast shipping.",positive
"Very disappointing, not as described.",negative
"Exceeded my expectations, will buy again.",positive
"Waste of money, do not recommend.",negative
"Happy with the purchase, five stars.",positive
"Poor customer service, will not return.",negative
code.explanation: A minimal CSV with balanced sentiment labels for demonstration.
code.best_practice_notes: Keep raw data in a dedicated `data/` folder; version control small CSVs, but ignore large datasets with .gitignore.

steps.title: Utility functions for loading data
steps.goal: Write reusable functions to read the CSV and split into train/test sets.
steps.speaker_notes: Let’s keep our code tidy by extracting data handling into a small utils module. Even though we said no classes, a plain Python file with functions works perfectly. In `src/utils.py` we’ll import pathlib’s Path, pandas, and scikit‑learn’s `train_test_split`. The `load_data` function takes a Path object, reads the CSV with `pd.read_csv`, and returns a DataFrame. The second function shuffles and splits the DataFrame into training and validation sets, returning four Series: X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test. Adding explicit type hints and a concise docstring makes the code self‑documenting and helps IDEs with autocomplete. After we write this file, we’ll be able to import `load_data` from both the training and inference scripts, ensuring we never duplicate logic.

code.code:
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from typing import Tuple

def load_data(csv_path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Read a CSV of reviews into a DataFrame.

    Parameters
    ----------
    csv_path: Path
        Path to the CSV file containing `text` and `label` columns.

    Returns
    -------
    pd.DataFrame
        DataFrame with raw data.
    """
    return pd.read_csv(csv_path)

def split_data(df: pd.DataFrame, test_ratio: float = 0.25) -> Tuple[pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series]:
    """Shuffle and split the DataFrame into train and test sets.

    Returns
    -------
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test as pandas Series.
    """
    X = df["text"]
    y = df["label"]
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
        X, y, test_size=test_ratio, random_state=42, stratify=y
    )
    return X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test
code.explanation: Utility functions centralize data loading and splitting, making the rest of the code cleaner.
code.best_practice_notes: Using pathlib for paths and explicit type hints improves portability and readability.

steps.title: Build the training script
steps.goal: Create a pipeline that vectorizes text and fits a logistic regression model.
bullets: Import utilities, sklearn Pipeline, TfidfVectorizer, LogisticRegression
Fit on training data and print accuracy on validation set
steps.speaker_notes: Now we get to the heart of the project – training a model. In `src/train.py` we’ll import the helper functions we just wrote, then build a scikit‑learn Pipeline. The pipeline first applies `TfidfVectorizer` to turn raw sentences into numeric features, then feeds those into a `LogisticRegression` classifier. Using a pipeline means we don’t have to manually transform the test set – the same vectorizer is applied automatically during prediction. After fitting, we evaluate on the hold‑out set with `accuracy_score` and print the result. This script will be executable from the command line with `python -m src.train`. By keeping everything functional and avoiding classes, the code stays short and beginner‑friendly while still demonstrating production‑grade composition.

code.code:
from pathlib import Path
import joblib
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

from utils import load_data, split_data

def main() -> None:
    data_path = Path(__file__).parent / "data" / "sample_reviews.csv"
    df = load_data(data_path)
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = split_data(df)

    # Build a simple pipeline: TF‑IDF → Logistic Regression
    model: Pipeline = Pipeline([
        ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(stop_words="english")),
        ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42))
    ])

    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    preds = model.predict(X_test)
    acc = accuracy_score(y_test, preds)
    print(f"Validation accuracy: {acc:.2%}")

    # Persist the trained pipeline for later inference
    model_path = Path(__file__).parent / "model.joblib"
    joblib.dump(model, model_path)
    print(f"Model saved to {model_path}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
code.explanation: Creates, trains, evaluates, and saves a scikit‑learn Pipeline using functional code only.
code.best_practice_notes: Persist the whole pipeline (vectorizer + classifier) with joblib so inference needs only one load.

steps.title: Run training and verify the model
steps.goal: Execute the training script, observe accuracy, and confirm model file creation.
steps.speaker_notes: Time to see the magic happen. First, make sure your virtual environment is active – if you used Poetry, run `poetry shell`. Then execute the training module with `python -m src.train`. The script will read the CSV, split the data, train the pipeline, and print the validation accuracy. With our tiny balanced dataset you should see 100 % accuracy – not because the model is perfect, but because the data is tiny and easy. After the run finishes, you’ll notice a new file called `model.joblib` sitting next to `train.py`. That binary contains the entire pipeline, ready for inference. If you don’t see the file, double‑check the path in the script and re‑run. This checkpoint confirms that our data pipeline, model, and persistence logic all work together.

steps.title: Create the inference script
steps.goal: Load the saved model and predict sentiment for user‑provided sentences.
steps.speaker_notes: Now that we have a trained model on disk, let’s write a tiny CLI that anyone can use. In `src/predict.py` we’ll import `argparse` to accept a free‑form sentence from the command line. The script loads `model.joblib` with joblib, calls `model.predict([sentence])`, and prints the result. Because the model is a full pipeline, we don’t need to manually vectorize – the pipeline handles it internally. I’ll also add a small helper function `predict_sentiment` that can be imported elsewhere if you ever want to build a web service. Remember to keep the code pure functions; the `if __name__ == "__main__"` block only deals with CLI plumbing.

code.explanation: CLI script that loads the persisted pipeline and predicts sentiment for a single input sentence.
code.expected_output: Sentiment: positive   # (or negative depending on input)
code.best_practice_notes: Separate pure prediction logic from CLI parsing to keep code reusable.

steps.title: Test the inference script with pytest
steps.goal: Write a unit test that loads the model and checks predictions on known inputs.
bullets: Use a fixture to load the model once
Assert predictions for two sample sentences
steps.speaker_notes: Testing is a crucial part of any AI project, even a tiny one. We’ll add a single pytest file that verifies our inference pipeline works as expected. First, we import the `load_model` and `predict_sentiment` functions from `src.predict`. Using a pytest fixture, we load the model once per test session – this avoids re‑reading the joblib file for each test, keeping the suite fast. Then we feed two known sentences: one clearly positive and one clearly negative, and assert that the predictions match the labels we used in the CSV. Run the suite with `pytest -q`; you should see two passed tests. If they fail, double‑check that you trained the model after any changes to the data or pipeline.

code.explanation: Two simple unit tests that ensure the persisted pipeline predicts correctly on known inputs.
code.best_practice_notes: Use module‑scoped fixtures to load heavy artifacts only once.

steps.title: Add a README and final polish
steps.goal: Document usage, installation, and provide a quick demo command.
bullets: Write a concise README with sections: Overview, Setup, Training, Inference, Testing
Add badges for Python version and build status (optional)
Include a one‑liner to run inference: `python -m src.predict "Your text"`
steps.speaker_notes: We’re almost done – a good project always ships with clear documentation. Open `README.md` and add a short overview of what the repo does, followed by a Setup section that tells viewers to run `poetry install` (or `pip install -e .`). Then describe how to train (`python -m src.train`) and how to predict (`python -m src.predict "I love it"`). I’ll also add a quick badge that shows we’re using Python 3.12. Once the README is saved, stage everything and make a final commit. This step is optional for the code but essential for anyone who clones the repo later. With the README in place, the tutorial is complete – you now have a fully functional, test‑covered AI project that you can extend however you like.

code.code:
# Custom Sentiment AI

A tiny, beginner‑friendly project that trains a sentiment classifier on a CSV of short reviews using **scikit‑learn**, **pandas**, and **joblib**. The code follows modern Python practices (pyproject.toml, src layout, type hints) and includes a pytest suite.

---

## Setup
```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/yourname/custom_sentiment_ai.git
cd custom_sentiment_ai

# Install dependencies (using Poetry or pip)
poetry install   # or: pip install -e .
```

## Train the model
```bash
python -m src.train
```
This will read `src/data/sample_reviews.csv`, train a TF‑IDF + Logistic Regression pipeline, print validation accuracy, and save `src/model.joblib`.

## Predict sentiment
```bash
python -m src.predict "I love this product!"
```
Output example:
```
Sentiment: positive
```

## Run tests
```bash
pytest -q
```
All tests should pass.

---

### License
MIT © Your Name
code.explanation: Provides clear instructions for installation, training, inference, and testing.
code.best_practice_notes: A good README turns a demo into a reusable template.

outro_transcript: Congratulations! You’ve just built a complete, production‑ready AI pipeline from scratch using only functions, pandas, and scikit‑learn. We set up a modern Python project with pyproject.toml, organized our code in a src layout, trained a TF‑IDF + Logistic Regression model, persisted it with joblib, and wrapped everything in a tiny command‑line tool. On top of that we added a pytest suite to keep our predictions trustworthy. From here you can experiment by adding more data, swapping the classifier for a linear SVM, or even exposing the model via a FastAPI endpoint. The sky’s the limit, but the core ideas – clean project structure, pipelines for composition, and reproducible testing – will serve you well in any future AI project. If you liked this video, hit the like button, subscribe for more hands‑on tutorials, and let me know in the comments what you’d like to build next. Thanks for watching, and happy coding!
final_project_code:
{'language': 'python', 'file_path': 'src/', 'code': '# Combined view of the final project files

# src/utils.py
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from typing import Tuple

def load_data(csv_path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Read a CSV of reviews into a DataFrame."""
    return pd.read_csv(csv_path)

def split_data(df: pd.DataFrame, test_ratio: float = 0.25) -> Tuple[pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series]:
    X = df["text"]
    y = df["label"]
    return train_test_split(X, y, test_size=test_ratio, random_state=42, stratify=y)

# src/train.py
from pathlib import Path
import joblib
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

from utils import load_data, split_data

def main() -> None:
    data_path = Path(__file__).parent / "data" / "sample_reviews.csv"
    df = load_data(data_path)
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = split_data(df)

    model: Pipeline = Pipeline([
        ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(stop_words="english")),
        ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42))
    ])
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    preds = model.predict(X_test)
    print(f"Validation accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, preds):.2%}")
    model_path = Path(__file__).parent / "model.joblib"
    joblib.dump(model, model_path)
    print(f"Model saved to {model_path}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# src/predict.py
import argparse
from pathlib import Path
import joblib
from typing import List

def load_model(model_path: Path):
    return joblib.load(model_path)

def predict_sentiment(model, sentences: List[str]):
    return model.predict(sentences)

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Predict sentiment of a sentence.")
    parser.add_argument("sentence", type=str, help="Sentence to classify")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    model = load_model(Path(__file__).parent / "model.joblib")
    print(f"Sentiment: {predict_sentiment(model, [args.sentence])[0]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# tests/test_predict.py
import pytest
from pathlib import Path
from src.predict import load_model, predict_sentiment

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def model():
    return load_model(Path(__file__).parent.parent / "src" / "model.joblib")

def test_positive_prediction(model):
    assert predict_sentiment(model, ["I love this!"])[0] == "positive"

def test_negative_prediction(model):
    assert predict_sentiment(model, ["Terrible experience."])[0] == "negative"
'}

challenges.title: Add hyperparameter tuning with GridSearchCV
challenges.description: Explore different regularization strengths for LogisticRegression and report the best configuration.
challenges.build_process: ['Replace the model definition in `src/train.py` with the GridSearchCV block above.', 'Re‑run `python -m src.train` to see the tuned results.']
challenges.solution_code:
{'language': 'python', 'file_path': 'src/train.py', 'code': 'from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Inside main(), replace model definition with a GridSearchCV wrapper
param_grid = {"clf__C": [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10]}
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(stop_words="english")),
    ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42))
])
search = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid, cv=3, scoring="accuracy")
search.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"Best C: {search.best_params_['clf__C']}")
print(f"Best CV accuracy: {search.best_score_:.2%}")
# Use the best estimator for final evaluation
best_model = search.best_estimator_
preds = best_model.predict(X_test)
print(f"Test accuracy with best model: {accuracy_score(y_test, preds):.2%}")
# Save best_model instead of the plain pipeline
joblib.dump(best_model, model_path)
', 'explanation': 'Uses GridSearchCV to find the optimal regularization parameter C for LogisticRegression.', 'expected_output': 'Best C: 1\nBest CV accuracy: 100.00%\nTest accuracy with best model: 100.00%\nModel saved to .../model.joblib', 'best_practice_notes': 'Wrap the pipeline in GridSearchCV to keep preprocessing inside cross‑validation.'}
